metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define `evidence_ref` — specify its type (string? nullable? structured?), what it references (e.g., the triggering sidecar row ID, a log correlation ID, or omit the field entirely if not yet designed), and add an AC assertion if it is required.
evidence: 'error{cause,evidence_ref} object' named in What but no definition of what evidence_ref contains or references
AC1 only checks `error.cause` non-empty; `evidence_ref` is not validated at all
ABSENCE: no schema for evidence_ref (e.g., a log-line ID, a sidecar row key, a free string) in any section
findings.finding: The `error` object schema includes `evidence_ref` (named in the What section) but its content, format, and whether it is required or optional are never defined, and the ACs do not validate it — leaving the executor to invent a schema.
findings.location: What section — 'error{cause,evidence_ref} object'; Acceptance Criteria
scenarios: Executor omits evidence_ref entirely (AC still passes), or invents a format that consumers cannot parse
A future reader of the sidecar cannot interpret evidence_ref because no canonical meaning was recorded
findings.suggested_fix: Either: (a) add 'evidence_ref: nullable string — the row_id of the most recent REVIEW_RESULT or COMPLETION_VERDICT sidecar at the moment of failure; null if unavailable' and add `jq -e '.error.evidence_ref != null'` (or `.error.evidence_ref | type == "string"`) to AC1; or (b) drop evidence_ref from the spec until it is designed.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify and clarify that `completion_sidecar.py`'s `build_payload` does NOT currently carry a `metrics` block (only `plan_review/sidecar.py` does), and update the Approach section to specify where the `error{}` object lands in each sidecar independently rather than relying on a symmetric 'alongside metrics' premise.
evidence: completion_sidecar.py build_payload (lines 182–218): payload keys are schema, verdict, ticket_id, findings/criteria, remediation, certifiable, runner, model, material_fingerprint — no 'metrics' key in either branch
plan_review/sidecar.py build_payload line 497: '"metrics": (verdict.get("coverage", {}) or {}).get("metrics", {})' — metrics present here only
Plan assertion: 'already carry a metrics block — the natural home, so no new event type is introduced'
findings.finding: The plan asserts both `REVIEW_RESULT`/`COMPLETION_VERDICT` sidecars 'already carry a `metrics` block' and calls them 'the natural home' for the new `error{}` object — but `completion_sidecar.py`'s `build_payload` contains no `metrics` field in either the SCHEMA (FAIL) or SCHEMA_PASS payloads. The assertion is only true of `plan_review/sidecar.py`. The plan's premise that the completion sidecar is a symmetric peer carrying metrics is unverified.
findings.location: Approach section: 'The REVIEW_RESULT/COMPLETION_VERDICT sidecars are non-replay events that survive compaction and already carry a metrics block — the natural home'
scenarios: The implementer follows the plan and adds the ERROR object 'alongside the metrics block' in completion_sidecar.py build_payload — but since there is no metrics block there, the structural assumption is wrong, and the resulting schema shape may be inconsistent with what was designed.

The plan's argument that ERROR belongs in the existing sidecars partly rests on symmetry with a metrics block that only exists in one of the two named files — a reader building on this premise may produce an inconsistent completion ERROR record.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'already carry a metrics block — the natural home' with separate statements: plan_review sidecar carries `metrics` (verified); completion sidecar has no `metrics` block and the ERROR payload placement must be specified explicitly for each file.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the schema tag for the ERROR payload and enumerate whether `latest_review_result` / `all_review_results` / `latest_review_timestamp` in `plan_review/sidecar.py` need schema-guard updates to either accept or deliberately skip ERROR records.
evidence: plan_review/sidecar.py:149-152: schema guard `payload.get('schema') in ('plan_review_result_v1', 'plan_review_result_v2')` — any other schema is silently skipped.
plan_review/sidecar.py:196-200: same guard in all_review_results().
Approach section: 'no new event type is introduced' — confirmed reuse of REVIEW_RESULT event type.
Plan is silent on the schema tag name for the ERROR payload and does not enumerate latest_review_result / all_review_results / latest_review_timestamp as affected consumers.
findings.finding: The schema-guarded readers in plan_review/sidecar.py (latest_review_result, all_review_results) only accept schema values 'plan_review_result_v1'/'plan_review_result_v2'; an ERROR record written to REVIEW_RESULT with a distinct or unspecified schema will be silently skipped by these readers. The plan does not specify the schema tag for the ERROR payload, nor enumerate these readers as consumers that may need updating.
findings.location: Files section: `src/rebar/llm/plan_review/sidecar.py` — add optional ERROR verdict + error object to payload builders.
scenarios: If the ERROR sidecar is written as a REVIEW_RESULT event with a new schema tag (e.g. 'plan_review_error_v1'), latest_review_result() skips it and returns the prior PASS/BLOCK record instead — the ERROR interval is invisible to any reader calling latest_review_result().

If the ERROR sidecar reuses schema 'plan_review_result_v2', latest_review_result() will return it as if it were a normal review result — the 'findings'/'verdict' fields will be absent or malformed for downstream consumers like attest.py and orchestrator.py.
findings.suggested_fix: Either (a) introduce a distinct schema tag (e.g. `plan_review_error_v1`) and confirm the existing schema guards correctly skip it (readers see no ERROR rows — intentional), or (b) add that tag to the guard list and define what the ERROR payload looks like when returned. Document the choice explicitly in the Approach section.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Enumerate `latest_fail_verdict()`, `latest_pass_record()`, and `audit/read.py` as consumers of `COMPLETION_VERDICT` events and clarify whether ERROR records under this event type should be visible or invisible to those readers (update schema guards accordingly).
evidence: completion_sidecar.py:125: `payload.get('schema') == SCHEMA` (SCHEMA='completion_verifier_fail_v1') — strict equality guard in latest_fail_verdict().
completion_sidecar.py:169: `payload.get('schema') == SCHEMA_PASS` (SCHEMA_PASS='completion_verifier_pass_v1') — strict equality guard in latest_pass_record().
audit/read.py:71-79: calls both latest_pass_record() and latest_fail_verdict() — also unenumerated.
Plan does not name latest_fail_verdict, latest_pass_record, or audit/read.py as consumers to review.
findings.finding: The plan adds the ERROR outcome to completion_sidecar.py's payload builder, but does not enumerate latest_fail_verdict() and latest_pass_record() — both of which are schema-guarded to 'completion_verifier_fail_v1'/'completion_verifier_pass_v1' — as consumers to evaluate. An ERROR record under COMPLETION_VERDICT with a different or unspecified schema will be silently skipped by both readers.
findings.location: Files section: `src/rebar/llm/completion_sidecar.py` — add optional ERROR verdict + error object to payload builders.
scenarios: An ERROR sidecar written to COMPLETION_VERDICT is silently skipped by both readers; the infra-failure interval is written but not queryable via the existing reader API.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Reconcile the 'write before re-raising' mechanism with the plan-review/code-review infra-exception paths, which catch `LLMUnavailableError` and RETURN an INDETERMINATE verdict rather than re-raising — specify the correct insertion point (e.g. inside or immediately before `_degraded_plan_review_verdict`) for each gate.
evidence: Plan states: 'on infra, write an ERROR sidecar before re-raising'
gate_dispatch.py line 140-142: `except LLMUnavailableError as exc: return _degraded_plan_review_verdict(...)` — caught and returned, not re-raised
gate_dispatch.py line 177-180: same pattern, mid-run LLMUnavailableError also returns INDETERMINATE
gate_dispatch.py lines 536-537 and 609-610: code-review paths also catch-and-return
gate_dispatch.py line 652: `runner_sel.preflight()` — only completion gate propagates
findings.finding: The plan's core mechanism — 'write an ERROR sidecar before re-raising' — does not match the actual plan-review and code-review gate control flow, where infra exceptions are caught and RETURNED as INDETERMINATE verdicts, not re-raised. Only the completion gate propagates the exception. The implementer following the plan's 'before re-raising' framing will look for a try/except…raise site that does not exist on the plan-review path, leading to either a misplaced write or a missed write.
findings.location: ## Approach / gate_dispatch.py lines 138-143, 177-180 (plan-review catches LLMUnavailableError and returns INDETERMINATE, never re-raises); lines 652-682 (completion propagates)
scenarios: Implementer adds the ERROR sidecar write inside a try/except…raise block that doesn't exist on the plan-review path, missing all plan-review infra failures
Implementer adds the write to only the completion path (where re-raise IS present), silently skipping plan-review and code-review infra errors
findings.suggested_fix: For plan-review: write the ERROR sidecar at the top of `_degraded_plan_review_verdict` (before partitioning/returning), passing `ctx.ticket_id` as the ticket anchor. For code-review: write inside `_degraded_code_review_verdict` when `request.target_ticket` is non-None (no ticket, no sidecar). For completion: write before the `raise LLMError(...)` at line 679, using `ticket_id`. The plan should describe these three distinct insertion points rather than a single 'before re-raising' framing.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define `error.evidence_ref`'s semantics: specify what it references (e.g. the sanitized diagnostic dict from `failure.outcome_of(exc).diagnostic`, or null when none), or drop the field if it has no defined referent.
evidence: Plan: 'an error{cause,evidence_ref} object'
ABSENCE: no definition of evidence_ref anywhere in plan, test spec, or AC
The existing `failure.py` produces a `diagnostic` dict and `resolution_fields`; neither is referred to as 'evidence_ref'
findings.finding: `error.evidence_ref` is named but never defined: the plan does not specify what artifact it references (a sidecar event id, a log URI, the `diagnostic` dict from `LLMOutcome`, a coverage field, or something else), making the field semantically unimplementable.
findings.location: Approach section / 'error{cause,evidence_ref} object'
scenarios: Implementer omits the field (leaving it null), but consumers interpret null as 'no evidence collected' vs 'schema version before evidence_ref existed'
Implementer uses a sidecar event filename; another implementer uses a coverage key — field is inconsistent across gates

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the schema tag for the ERROR sidecar payload (e.g. `'plan_review_result_error_v1'` / `'completion_verifier_error_v1'`) and specify whether `latest_review_result` / `latest_fail_verdict` should skip it (add to schema guard deny-list) or return it (update schema guard accept-list), to prevent silent read breakage.
evidence: plan_review/sidecar.py:149-151: schema guard accepts only 'plan_review_result_v1'/'plan_review_result_v2' — ERROR record with a new tag is silently skipped
completion_sidecar.py:125: schema guard `payload.get('schema') == SCHEMA` — ERROR record skipped unless it matches
Plan: 'no new event type is introduced' — implies same EVENT_TYPE, same ticket dir, same reader path
ABSENCE: no new schema constant, no reader update, no specification of which schema tag the ERROR record carries
findings.finding: The schema tag for the ERROR sidecar record is unspecified. Both sidecar modules apply schema-string guards on reads (`'plan_review_result_v2'` in `latest_review_result`; `SCHEMA`/`SCHEMA_PASS` in `latest_fail_verdict`/`latest_pass_record`). If the ERROR record reuses an existing schema tag, existing readers will return it unexpectedly (callers never expect `verdict='ERROR'`); if it uses a new schema tag, existing readers will silently skip it — neither outcome is specified and no reader update is mentioned.
findings.location: Files section / sidecar.py, completion_sidecar.py; Approach section / 'no new event type is introduced'
scenarios: An offline tool calls latest_review_result() expecting the most-recent review — instead gets the ERROR record if it reuses v2, with an unexpected verdict='ERROR' field
A diagnosis query for ERROR records via jq finds zero results because all ERROR records are written with a new unregistered schema tag that readers skip
findings.suggested_fix: Use a distinct schema tag (e.g. `plan_review_result_error_v1`) and update the schema guards in both readers to explicitly skip it, so they continue returning only PASS/BLOCK/FAIL records while the ERROR record is separately queryable by a new reader or direct jq.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define `error.cause`'s value domain: specify whether it is a free-form string (e.g. `str(exc)`), a `ResolutionClass` enum value from `failure.py`, or a structured sub-object — and state what value is written when the exception carries no `.outcome` (e.g. a raw non-LLM tooling error).
evidence: Plan: 'an error{cause,evidence_ref} object on the gates' infrastructure-exception path'

ResolutionClass in failure.py already provides an 8-value closed vocabulary (WAIT_AND_RETRY, RETRY_NOW, …, NEEDS_INVESTIGATION) that the existing infra-failure path uses — plan does not say whether cause uses this or str(exc)
ABSENCE: no definition of cause's type, vocabulary, or nullability anywhere in the plan
findings.finding: The `error.cause` field's value domain is never defined: it is unclear whether `cause` is a free-form string (e.g. `str(exc)`), a `ResolutionClass` enum value (the 8-class vocabulary already in `failure.py`), or a structured dict. An implementer cannot determine the type/vocabulary from the plan alone.
findings.location: Approach section / 'error{cause,evidence_ref} object'; Files: src/rebar/llm/completion_sidecar.py, src/rebar/llm/plan_review/sidecar.py
scenarios: Implementer writes cause=str(exc), but offline consumers expect a ResolutionClass string for programmatic routing — the field is silently unqueryable
cause is left empty on a non-LLMUnavailableError 'tooling' infra failure that has no outcome attribute, breaking the non-empty AC assertion
findings.suggested_fix: Specify: `cause` = `str(exc)` (human-readable, always non-empty); optionally add `resolution_class` as a parallel field carrying the `ResolutionClass.value` string when `failure.outcome_of(exc)` is non-None.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the classification boundary: enumerate which exception types (e.g. `LLMUnavailableError` and its subclasses, plus optionally `WorkflowError`) trigger ERROR sidecar emission, and confirm that a normal domain BLOCK/FAIL verdict path (which raises no exception) never reaches the ERROR branch.
evidence: Plan: 'classify an infra exception (provider 5xx/connection/tooling) vs a domain BLOCK/FAIL'
Existing code: LLMUnavailableError is the established infra-failure exception; gate_dispatch.py already catches it at 4 sites and routes to INDETERMINATE
failure.py defines ResolutionClass with 8 values — but plan does not specify which map to ERROR
ABSENCE: no mapping table from exception type/class to ERROR vs no-ERROR decision
findings.finding: The infra-vs-domain classification rule is stated in the `gate_dispatch.py` files entry but not co-located with the payload schema defined in `sidecar.py` / `completion_sidecar.py`, and the boundary is not specified: the plan does not state which exception types or `ResolutionClass` values constitute 'infra' (triggering ERROR) vs 'domain BLOCK/FAIL' (no ERROR). `LLMUnavailableError` is the obvious candidate, but 'tooling' failures that are plain `Exception` subclasses are also listed.
findings.location: Files section / gate_dispatch.py; Approach section / 'classify an infra exception (provider 5xx/connection/tooling) vs a domain BLOCK/FAIL'
scenarios: A WorkflowError (YAML parse failure, a 'tooling' failure) is raised mid-gate: should it produce an ERROR sidecar? The plan says yes ('tooling') but the code has no such path
A domain BLOCK verdict that also triggers an internal exception causes confusion about which branch fires
findings.suggested_fix: Define: ERROR is written iff the gate raises `LLMUnavailableError` (or a listed superset); a domain BLOCK/FAIL returns a verdict dict normally and raises nothing, so it trivially produces no ERROR.

evidence: 1 criterion line(s) bundle multiple deliverables with 'and' (split so each is independently verifiable).
findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Either drop `evidence_ref` from the `error` object (it has no consumer in the ACs or tests) or define concretely what it refers to, what type it carries, and which consumer reads it.
evidence: Plan quote: 'an additive `verdict="ERROR"` outcome with an `error{cause,evidence_ref}` object'
AC: 'persisted sidecar with `jq -e .verdict=="ERROR"` and non-empty `error.cause`' — evidence_ref never appears in ACs
No existing `evidence_ref` field anywhere in the codebase (search confirms absence)
No consumer of `evidence_ref` named in the plan
findings.finding: The `evidence_ref` field in `error{cause, evidence_ref}` has no named consumer in the plan, no definition of what it refers to (a ticket id? a log line? a sidecar filename?), and no existing parallel in the codebase. It is YAGNI: the plan's Acceptance Criteria only assert `error.cause` is non-empty — `evidence_ref` is never mentioned in the ACs or tests.
findings.location: ## What — 'error{cause,evidence_ref} object'; AC lines assert only `error.cause` non-empty
scenarios: The field ships as always-null or always-empty, becoming dead payload weight.
A future implementer invents a meaning for `evidence_ref` that conflicts with what the original author intended, since the intent is undocumented.
findings.suggested_fix: Drop `evidence_ref` from the payload shape; if a future consumer needs a log/sidecar cross-reference, add it then with a named consumer and a defined type.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify whether code-review infra errors without a `target_ticket` should emit an ERROR sidecar (they cannot — no ticket anchor) or be explicitly excluded, matching the existing code-review sidecar pattern of 'emit only when target_ticket is supplied'.
evidence: `CodeReviewRequest.target_ticket: str | None = None` — explicitly optional
Plan statement: 'Only gate-encountered infra failures are captured' — does not qualify whether ticket-less code-review runs are included or excluded
Existing code_review/sidecar.py already handles this by only emitting 'when a target_ticket is supplied'
findings.finding: The plan does not specify how the ERROR sidecar write handles the code-review gate's optional `target_ticket` (which is `None` on diff-only invocations). Writing a sidecar requires a ticket anchor; without one there is nowhere to emit it. The plan is silent on this edge case.
findings.location: ## Files / gate_dispatch.py `_degraded_code_review_verdict` / `CodeReviewRequest.target_ticket`
scenarios: A diff-only `rebar review-code` run hits a provider 5xx — no target_ticket, so no ERROR sidecar is writable; but the plan's AC ('yields a persisted sidecar') does not address this case
Implementer writes unconditionally and raises a KeyError/AttributeError when target_ticket is None
findings.suggested_fix: Add a sentence to the Approach: 'For the code-review gate, the ERROR sidecar is written only when `request.target_ticket` is non-None, matching the existing REVIEW_RESULT emit guard.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC (or extend AC1) to assert that `error.evidence_ref` is present/non-null when an infra exception produces an ERROR sidecar, or explicitly note in the plan that evidence_ref is optional and may be absent.
evidence: What section: 'an error{cause,evidence_ref} object'
AC1 only checks: 'non-empty error.cause' — evidence_ref is never referenced in any AC or Tests section
findings.finding: The `evidence_ref` field is named in the What section as part of the `error{cause,evidence_ref}` object but no acceptance criterion verifies it — it is an orphan described feature with no covering test assertion.
findings.location: What section ('error{cause,evidence_ref}') vs AC1 ('non-empty error.cause' only)
scenarios: An implementer omits evidence_ref entirely or sets it to null — no criterion fails, yet the described data contract is broken.
A consumer that reads evidence_ref to locate the related log event gets an absent field silently.
findings.suggested_fix: Extend AC1: '…non-empty error.cause, and error.evidence_ref is a non-empty string referencing the related log/event ID' — or add a sentence in Approach clarifying that evidence_ref is intentionally optional (and may be null) so implementers know the expected contract.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an update to AGENTS.md (or an ADR) capturing the rule: 'gate_dispatch must write an ERROR sidecar before re-raising any infra exception — agent self-report is not used for this path,' so future gate authors inherit the contract.
evidence: The Approach section articulates the decision rationale inline but names no documentation artifact (ADR, AGENTS.md, design doc) that would persist this contract for future contributors.
ABSENCE: no '## Documentation' section, no 'update AGENTS.md', no ADR reference anywhere in the plan.
findings.finding: The novel pattern 'gate code is responsible for stamping ERROR sidecars on infra failures; agent self-report must not be used' constitutes an architectural decision that future gate authors must follow, but the plan mentions no update to AGENTS.md, an ADR, or any design doc to capture this contract.
findings.location: ## Approach — 'Stamped by gate code on a real failure, so the self-report reliability problem does not apply'
scenarios: A new gate is added six months later; the author doesn't know they must call the ERROR sidecar builder on infra exceptions, so the diagnosis interval is never recorded — silently breaking the feature without any failing test.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a '## Documentation' section to the plan: 'Update AGENTS.md § Gate contracts: on any infra exception (provider 5xx / connection / tooling), gate_dispatch writes an ERROR sidecar before re-raising; self-report of infra failures is out of scope.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify in the Approach how repeated ERROR sidecars on retry are handled (e.g., include an attempt/invocation ID so consumers can deduplicate, or document that callers must not retry before the sidecar is read).
evidence: The Approach describes the interval model (ERROR→PASS) without mentioning how repeated ERROR sidecars on retry are handled or de-duplicated.
ABSENCE: no idempotency key, attempt counter, or deduplication strategy mentioned anywhere in the plan.
findings.finding: The plan does not address idempotency of ERROR sidecar writes: if the gate invocation is retried (by a retry loop in a caller), multiple ERROR sidecar records are written for the same logical gate attempt, potentially corrupting the diagnosis-interval timeline.
findings.location: ## Approach — 'a diagnosis interval starts (ERROR) and ends (next PASS) is recorded'
scenarios: A retry wrapper around gate_dispatch retries an infra failure three times; three ERROR sidecars are written with identical timestamps/gate IDs; downstream analytics counts three separate diagnosis intervals instead of one.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define and enforce idempotency for ERROR sidecar writes under retry — e.g. include a gate-invocation idempotency key in the sidecar payload and deduplicate on write (or document that callers must not retry after an ERROR sidecar is written).
evidence: 'write an ERROR sidecar before re-raising' — the plan re-raises the exception, making retry by the caller the common case for transient infra failures (connection errors are the stated example)
'a stubbed gate run raising a connection error writes a sidecar with verdict=ERROR' — unit test covers only a single failure, not repeated failures for the same gate invocation
T9 checklist: 'Shared/mutable state mutated atomically; operation idempotent under retry/at-least-once'
findings.finding: The plan does not specify whether ERROR sidecar writes are idempotent under retry. Gate code re-raises the infra exception after writing the sidecar; callers that retry on connection errors will cause multiple ERROR sidecars to be written for the same logical gate invocation. This can corrupt the diagnosis-interval semantics (ERROR→ERROR→PASS instead of ERROR→PASS) without any deduplication or idempotency key defined.
findings.location: ## Approach — 'Stamped by gate code on a real failure, so the self-report reliability problem does not apply.' / gate_dispatch.py
scenarios: Any caller that implements retry-on-connection-error (standard for provider 5xx/connection failures) will write N ERROR sidecars for a single failed gate invocation; downstream consumers computing diagnosis intervals will see a multi-ERROR run instead of a clean ERROR→PASS pair, silently corrupting interval metrics.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a `gate_invocation_id` (e.g. a UUID generated at gate-dispatch entry) to the ERROR sidecar payload. On write, check whether a sidecar with that id already exists and skip the write if so (or use an upsert). Document this in the AC and add a test that a gate raising twice produces exactly one ERROR sidecar.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm whether `error.cause` is genuinely additive over the already-persisted `coverage.llm_error` + `coverage.diagnostic`; if not, consider pointing consumers to `coverage.llm_error` / `coverage.resolution_class` instead of introducing a parallel field.
evidence: plan_review/sidecar.py build_payload line 497: `'coverage': verdict.get('coverage', {})` — the full coverage dict including llm_error/resolution_class/diagnostic is already persisted
gate_dispatch.py:417: `'llm_error': str(error)` already stamped on coverage of degraded verdicts
gate_dispatch.py:409-418: `resolution_fields(outcome)` already writes `resolution_class`, `retryable`, `diagnostic` onto coverage — all persisted via the existing sidecar coverage field
Plan phrase: 'add an optional `ERROR` verdict + `error` object to the payload builders' — no rationale for why coverage.llm_error is insufficient for the cause field
findings.finding: The proposed `error.cause` field in the new `error{cause, evidence_ref}` object rebuilds information already persisted by the existing infra-failure pipeline: `coverage.llm_error` (str(error), written at gate_dispatch.py lines 417 and 472) and `coverage.diagnostic` (sanitized dict from `failure.resolution_fields`) are already lifted verbatim into the sidecar payload's `coverage` key by both `plan_review/sidecar.py:build_payload` and `completion_sidecar.py:build_payload`. Adding a second `error.cause` carrier duplicates `coverage.llm_error` without explaining why the existing field is insufficient or what the new top-level `error` object offers that querying `coverage.llm_error` does not.
findings.location: ## Approach / Files: `src/rebar/llm/plan_review/sidecar.py`, `src/rebar/llm/completion_sidecar.py` — `build_payload`
scenarios: An offline consumer reading a sidecar with `verdict=ERROR` finds both `error.cause` and `coverage.llm_error` carrying the same string, creating confusion about which is canonical.
A future schema reader must handle two overlapping error-description fields with no documented relationship.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the classification rule explicitly — e.g., name the exception classes (or base class) that constitute 'infra' (e.g., `ProviderConnectionError`, `HTTPStatusError` with 5xx) vs domain (`BlockVerdict`, `FailVerdict`) so the executor has no design choice to make.
evidence: 'classify an infra exception (provider 5xx/connection/tooling) vs a domain BLOCK/FAIL' — no exception class names, status-code ranges, or lookup table cited
ABSENCE: no exception taxonomy or classification helper is referenced in any of the three named files
findings.finding: The classification rule distinguishing an 'infra exception' (provider 5xx/connection/tooling) from a 'domain BLOCK/FAIL' is named but the mechanism is unspecified — the executor must decide whether to key on exception class, HTTP status code, a registry, or something else.
findings.location: Files section — `gate_dispatch.py` bullet; Approach section
scenarios: Executor picks a classification strategy that diverges from author intent, shipping false-positive or false-negative ERROR sidecars
findings.suggested_fix: Add a sentence such as: 'Infra exceptions are identified by catching `ProviderConnectionError | httpx.HTTPStatusError` (status ≥ 500) before catching `GateDomainError`; anything else re-raises unchanged.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify (in the Approach or gate_dispatch implementation note) that the ERROR sidecar write is wrapped in a try/except that logs-and-suppresses write failures, so the original infra exception always propagates unmodified.
evidence: 'write an ERROR sidecar before re-raising' — the plan says nothing about wrapping the sidecar write in a try/except or logging-and-suppressing its failure.
ABSENCE: no mention of error handling for the sidecar write itself in the Approach, Tests, or AC sections.
findings.finding: The plan introduces a sidecar write operation inside an exception handler, but does not specify behavior if that write itself fails — risking either masking the original infra exception or surfacing a confusing double-exception to callers.
findings.location: src/rebar/llm/workflow/gate_dispatch.py — 'on infra, write an ERROR sidecar before re-raising'
scenarios: Disk full or serialization error during sidecar write raises a second exception inside the except block, replacing the original connection error with an unrelated IOError — the caller's circuit-breaker/retry logic sees the wrong exception type.
In a test environment the sidecar path is misconfigured; every gate invocation now raises IOError instead of the expected provider exception, breaking unrelated tests.
findings.suggested_fix: In gate_dispatch.py's exception handler: `try: write_error_sidecar(...) except Exception as sidecar_exc: logger.warning('ERROR sidecar write failed: %s', sidecar_exc)` then unconditionally `raise` the original exception.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm `tests/unit/workflow/test_gate_error_sidecar.py` is treated as a *creation* target (it does not yet exist); no action needed beyond writing it, but ensure CI picks it up under the `tests/unit/workflow/` discovery glob.
evidence: Directory listing of tests/unit/workflow/ shows no test_gate_error_sidecar.py among its 30+ files.
Search for 'test_gate_error_sidecar' returns no matches in the repo.

Plan labels this file '— new', so its absence is expected as a to-be-created artifact — but it is listed in Files as a named edit target alongside existing files, which is correct for a new file. This is not a hallucinated existing file; it is a file the plan must CREATE.
findings.finding: tests/unit/workflow/test_gate_error_sidecar.py does not exist in the repository — the directory tests/unit/workflow/ exists and is populated, but this specific file is absent.
findings.location: Files section: `tests/unit/workflow/test_gate_error_sidecar.py` — new.
scenarios: Reviewer tooling that checks every named file in the Files section against the current repo will flag this as missing — the plan should clarify it is a creation, not a modification of an existing file.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explain in the plan why existing INDETERMINATE sidecars with `coverage.resolution_class` / `coverage.llm_error` (already emitted by `_degraded_plan_review_verdict` → `sidecar.emit`) are insufficient as the 'interval start' marker, or restructure the plan to extend those records with the `error{cause, evidence_ref}` object rather than adding a parallel `verdict='ERROR'` sidecar.
evidence: Plan states: 'so a diagnosis interval starts (ERROR) and ends (next PASS) is recorded by gate code'

`_degraded_plan_review_verdict` already calls `emit(verdict, ...)` after setting `coverage.llm_unavailable=True`, `coverage.resolution_class`, `coverage.diagnostic` — giving a queryable INDETERMINATE sidecar with structured infra-failure metadata
`failure.resolution_fields(outcome)` already persists `{resolution_class, retryable, diagnostic}` into every degraded sidecar's coverage block
ABSENCE: plan contains no discussion of why `jq '.verdict=="INDETERMINATE" and .coverage.resolution_class'` on existing sidecars does not already bound the interval
findings.finding: The plan's ERROR sidecar is solving a problem (durable infra-failure record for interval tracking) that the existing `failure.py` classification machinery + the current degrade-path sidecar emission already address: degraded INDETERMINATE sidecars already carry `coverage.resolution_class`, `coverage.retryable`, `coverage.diagnostic`, and `coverage.llm_error`. The plan does not engage with this prior art or explain why querying INDETERMINATE sidecars with `resolution_class` set is insufficient as the 'interval start' marker.
findings.location: ## Approach section; src/rebar/llm/failure.py (`resolution_fields`, `LLMOutcome`); src/rebar/llm/workflow/gate_dispatch.py (`_degraded_plan_review_verdict`)
scenarios: The ERROR sidecar is shipped; offline analysts discover the INDETERMINATE-with-resolution_class records already cover the same interval; two partially-redundant records now coexist with no dedup/precedence rule
The team defers ERROR work because the prior art is discovered mid-implementation, creating scope churn

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify (and document in the plan) that `ticket_id` is in scope at the completion gate's infra-exception hook point in `gate_dispatch.py`, and specify which exception types on that path are classified 'infra' vs. 'domain' (the completion gate raises `LLMError` generically — clarify how the classifier distinguishes the two for that gate).
evidence: Plan: 'classify an infra exception (provider 5xx/connection/tooling) vs a domain BLOCK/FAIL; on infra, write an ERROR sidecar before re-raising'
`produce_completion_verdict` raises `LLMError` on mid-run failure (line 679) — no sidecar.emit call exists on any completion failure path
`produce_completion_verdict` preflight also raises `LLMUnavailableError` before `ticket_id` appears in a `with gate_config(cfg):` block — sidecar routing requires `ticket_id` from the caller's scope
ABSENCE: plan contains no verified statement that `ticket_id` is accessible at the completion gate's re-raise hook
findings.finding: The plan asserts an ERROR sidecar can be written 'before re-raising' on the completion gate's infra-exception path, but `produce_completion_verdict` currently raises `LLMError` on failure with no sidecar emission at all. It is unverified that `ticket_id` (required for sidecar routing via `append_event(ticket_id, ...)`) is reliably in scope at the `gate_dispatch` hook point for the completion gate's pre-raise path, and the plan does not specify which exception types on the completion path qualify as 'infra' vs. domain failures.
findings.location: ## Files section (gate_dispatch.py); src/rebar/llm/workflow/gate_dispatch.py `produce_completion_verdict` (lines 634-683)
scenarios: Implementation begins; the completion gate's pre-raise path lacks ticket_id context or has no suitable emit call site; the ERROR sidecar is silently skipped or requires a larger refactor than scoped

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Assert the post-rollback schema state explicitly: confirm that already-persisted ERROR sidecar records are safe for all existing consumers after rollback (i.e., the additive field is ignored by rolled-back readers), or add a pruning/cleanup step if not.
evidence: Plan states: 'back-out = stop writing ERROR' — describes the write-stop action only

No assertion that previously-persisted ERROR records are harmless, prunable, or that downstream consumers (compaction, analytics, replay) are safe when they encounter ERROR records after rollback to a version that never wrote them
T11 rule: 'the plan must assert on the schema STATE AFTER rollback, not merely that rollback exits 0'
findings.finding: The rollback description ('back-out = stop writing ERROR') states the action but does not assert the schema STATE of already-written ERROR sidecar records after rollback — T11 requires an explicit assertion on post-rollback state, not merely that rollback exits cleanly.
findings.location: Approach section — 'back-out = stop writing ERROR'
scenarios: A rollback is performed after N ERROR sidecars have been written; a consumer added during the ERROR-writing window now reads ERROR records from a codebase that no longer understands them — plan is silent on whether this is safe or requires pruning
findings.suggested_fix: Add one sentence to the Approach section: 'After rollback (stop writing ERROR), any already-persisted ERROR sidecar records are inert — existing readers ignore unknown verdict values and the error object, so no pruning is required and the schema state is equivalent to pre-feature for all live consumers.'

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
